enrichment.short_description: All-in-one cloud HCM platform from ADP combining payroll, HR, benefits, time tracking, and talent management for mid-sized employers.
enrichment.starting_price: Not publicly listed; ADP quotes custom pricing per employee per month based on company size and selected modules (third-party estimates commonly cite roughly $23-$30 PEPM plus a one-time setup fee)

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ADP Workforce Now is a cloud-based human capital management (HCM) platform from ADP, one of the largest and longest-running payroll and HR services providers in the world. It brings payroll, HR administration, time and attendance, benefits administration, talent management, and workforce analytics together in a single underlying database, so employee data entered once can flow through every module without manual re-entry. The platform is aimed squarely at mid-sized organizations, roughly 50 to 1,000+ employees, while ADP offers separate products (such as RUN Powered by ADP and its larger enterprise HCM suite) for smaller and much larger employers respectively.

Because Workforce Now is delivered as configurable packages, commonly marketed under names like Select, Plus, and Premium, rather than a single one-size-fits-all subscription, buyers choose the modules and level of service they need and add more as the company grows. ADP layers in tax filing, compliance expertise, and dedicated implementation support on top of the software, positioning Workforce Now less as pure self-serve SaaS and more as a managed HR and payroll service backed by a software platform.

In recent years ADP has added ADP Assist, a generative-AI chat assistant embedded across the product, along with expanded self-service tools and analytics, reflecting an effort to modernize the interface and reduce the manual administrative burden traditionally associated with legacy ADP systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who ADP Workforce Now Is For
paragraphs: Workforce Now fits best for growing mid-market companies, typically in the 50-to-1,000-employee range, that have outgrown a basic payroll tool or a smaller ADP product and need integrated benefits administration, time tracking, and HR compliance support in one place. It suits multi-state or multi-location employers who need help navigating varying state and local tax and labor rules, since ADP's compliance and tax-filing expertise is one of the platform's core selling points.

It's also a strong fit for HR teams that prefer a single vendor relationship with dedicated implementation and support staff over a fully self-serve, do-it-yourself SaaS tool, and for organizations in industries such as retail, healthcare, manufacturing, and professional services, where ADP has deep, long-standing experience and pre-built compliance content.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Very small businesses and solo employers are usually better served by ADP's own lighter-weight RUN Powered by ADP product, or by simpler, cheaper payroll tools, since Workforce Now's package structure, setup fees, and implementation process are built around mid-sized company complexity rather than a handful of employees.

Buyers who want fully transparent, instantly visible, self-service pricing may find the sales process frustrating, since ADP does not publish exact rates and typically requires a custom quote and contract negotiation. Companies looking for a lightweight, modern point solution for a single function, like standalone time tracking or a specialized applicant tracking system, may also be better off with a best-of-breed tool, since Workforce Now's strength is breadth and integration rather than being the deepest option in any one category. Organizations with very large, complex global payroll footprints across many countries are also usually steered by ADP toward its enterprise-tier HCM products instead of Workforce Now.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Workforce Now centralizes payroll processing and tax filing, automating calculations and filings across federal, state, and local jurisdictions while flagging anomalies before a payroll run is finalized. HR management tools handle employee records, org charts, onboarding workflows, and compliance tracking, all fed by the same employee database used by payroll.

Benefits administration connects to a large network of insurance carriers, letting HR configure plans while giving employees a guided, mobile-friendly enrollment experience. Time and attendance functionality covers scheduling, time capture, and PTO tracking with configurable rules for overtime and labor law compliance, and optional talent modules extend into recruiting, onboarding, and performance management for companies that want those functions on the same platform.

Analytics and reporting tools turn the combined payroll, HR, and time data into benchmarking and workforce insights, and ADP Assist, ADP's generative-AI assistant, is layered across these modules to answer HR questions, surface anomalies, and reduce time spent on routine administrative tasks.

long_form_blocks.heading: The ADP Workforce Now Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, HR admins and managers work from a web dashboard that surfaces payroll, time, and HR tasks in one place, while employees get a companion mobile app for self-service tasks like checking pay stubs, requesting time off, and enrolling in benefits. ADP has invested in modernizing this interface over recent years, though some longtime users and reviewers still describe parts of the admin experience, particularly deeper configuration screens and custom reporting, as dense or dated compared to newer, mobile-first HR startups.

Implementation is typically a guided, multi-week process run with a dedicated ADP team, which many mid-sized employers value for compliance confidence but which also means Workforce Now is not something a company can self-serve and launch in an afternoon. Ongoing support is usually delivered through named service representatives or support tiers tied to the chosen package, a model closer to a managed HR service than a purely self-service SaaS product.

Overall, the experience trades some interface polish and pricing transparency for deep payroll and compliance expertise, broad module coverage, and the reassurance of working with one of the industry's most established HR and payroll providers.

features.title: Payroll processing & tax filing
features.description: Automated payroll calculations with built-in federal, state, and local tax filing, plus alerts that flag anomalies before a pay run is submitted.

features.title: HR management (HRIS)
features.description: A central employee record system covering onboarding, org charts, document management, and labor law compliance tracking.

features.title: Benefits administration
features.description: Plan setup and management with connectivity to a broad network of insurance carriers and a guided employee enrollment experience.

features.title: Time and attendance
features.description: Scheduling, time capture, and PTO tracking with configurable rules for overtime and jurisdiction-specific labor compliance.

features.title: Talent acquisition & management
features.description: Optional recruiting, onboarding, and performance management modules that plug into the same employee database as payroll and HR.

features.title: Workforce analytics & reporting
features.description: Reporting and benchmarking tools that turn combined payroll, HR, and time data into workforce insights for decision-making.

features.title: ADP Assist (AI)
features.description: A generative-AI chat assistant embedded across the platform that answers HR questions and helps surface data-driven insights.

features.title: Employee self-service & mobile app
features.description: A mobile app and portal letting employees view pay stubs, request time off, update information, and enroll in benefits.

features.title: Compliance support
features.description: Ongoing help navigating multi-state and multi-jurisdiction tax, labor, and regulatory requirements as part of the managed service.

features.title: Open API & integrations
features.description: APIs and a marketplace of pre-built integrations that connect Workforce Now to other business and finance systems.

pros: Combines payroll, HR, benefits, time tracking, and talent management in one integrated database, reducing duplicate data entry
Backed by ADP's decades of payroll tax and compliance expertise, useful for multi-state employers
Modular packages let companies start with core payroll/HR and add benefits, time, or talent modules as they grow
Established vendor with a large partner and integration ecosystem and dedicated implementation support
Mobile app and self-service portal give employees convenient access to pay, time off, and benefits
cons: Pricing is not published; buyers must go through a custom sales quote and negotiation process
Implementation and onboarding can take several weeks and typically requires a dedicated project, unlike instant-signup SaaS tools
Some admin screens and reporting tools are viewed by longtime users as dense or dated compared to newer HR platforms
Add-on modules and setup fees can make total cost less predictable than a flat per-user subscription
Overkill and cost-prohibitive for very small businesses better served by ADP's own lighter RUN product

faqs.question: What is ADP Workforce Now?
faqs.answer: ADP Workforce Now is a cloud-based human capital management (HCM) platform from ADP that combines payroll, HR administration, time and attendance, benefits administration, talent management, and analytics for mid-sized businesses.

faqs.question: How much does ADP Workforce Now cost?
faqs.answer: ADP does not publish fixed pricing. Costs are quoted per employee per month based on company size, selected modules, and contract terms, and typically include a one-time implementation or setup fee; third-party estimates commonly put base pricing in the roughly $23-$30 PEPM range.

faqs.question: Who is ADP Workforce Now designed for?
faqs.answer: It is designed primarily for mid-sized organizations, generally in the range of about 50 to 1,000+ employees, especially multi-state or multi-location businesses that need integrated payroll, HR, and compliance support.

faqs.question: Does ADP Workforce Now offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: ADP does not publicly advertise a self-service free trial for Workforce Now; prospective customers typically go through a sales consultation and demo to receive a custom quote.

faqs.question: What is ADP Assist?
faqs.answer: ADP Assist is ADP's generative-AI chat assistant embedded within Workforce Now, designed to answer HR and payroll questions, surface anomalies, and help admins and employees find information faster.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based practice management, EHR, and medical billing software for independent healthcare practices and billing companies.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing, typically quoted per provider per month; publicly cited starting points run from roughly $429/provider/month (practice management + billing) up to $729/provider/month (practice management + EHR)

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: AdvancedMD is a cloud-based, all-in-one medical software suite that combines practice management (PM), electronic health records (EHR), and medical billing/revenue cycle management (RCM) tools for outpatient healthcare practices and the billing companies that serve them. Founded in 1999 and headquartered in South Jordan, Utah, the company has changed ownership several times over the years — from Francisco Partners to ADP in 2011, to Global Payments in 2018, and back to Francisco Partners in a 2024 deal — while continuing to operate as a standalone, US-focused ambulatory healthcare IT platform.

The platform is built around a shared database that ties scheduling, clinical documentation, e-prescribing, claims submission, and patient engagement together rather than stitching together separate point solutions. That modular design is one of AdvancedMD's key differentiators: practices can license PM and billing alone, add EHR later, or buy the full suite, with everything sharing the same patient and financial records.

AdvancedMD markets itself to two overlapping audiences: independent physician practices — commonly ranging from solo providers to multi-provider groups — across a wide range of specialties, and medical billing companies or RCM service bureaus that manage claims for many client practices from a single account, a niche where AdvancedMD has a particularly strong reputation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who AdvancedMD Is For
paragraphs: AdvancedMD is best suited to small and mid-sized independent practices — from solo physicians to multi-provider groups — that want practice management, billing, and clinical charting handled by one contracted vendor rather than integrating separate best-of-breed tools. It supports a wide range of specialties, including primary care, behavioral health, physical therapy, chiropractic, and OB-GYN, through specialty-specific templates.

It is also a strong fit for medical billing companies and revenue-cycle outsourcing firms, since the platform was built to let a single billing service log into one account and manage claims, statements, and collections across many separate client practices without juggling a different software instance per client.

Practices that value phone-based onboarding and training, an established vendor with mature claims-clearinghouse relationships, and are comfortable with per-provider, per-module subscription pricing tend to be the happiest AdvancedMD customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Very small or cash-pay-only practices looking for the cheapest possible EHR or scheduling tool may find AdvancedMD's modular, per-provider pricing more expensive than lightweight competitors, especially once billing services, patient engagement, and telehealth add-ons are layered on top of the base subscription.

Practices that prioritize a modern, minimal-click interface should also be cautious: AdvancedMD's UI is frequently described in independent reviews as functional but dense and somewhat dated compared to newer, design-forward EHRs, and new staff typically need real training time before becoming fully productive in it.

Large hospital systems and enterprise health networks needing deep inpatient or multi-facility functionality are generally better served by enterprise-grade EHR platforms built for that scale — AdvancedMD is designed for ambulatory, outpatient practices rather than hospital-level deployments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, AdvancedMD bundles practice management (scheduling, patient registration, insurance eligibility checks), a certified EHR with e-prescribing and specialty-specific charting templates, and medical billing/RCM tools (claims scrubbing, clearinghouse submission, denial management, and collections reporting) into one connected system.

Patient engagement runs through a patient portal, automated appointment reminders, and online intake or check-in, and the company added native telemedicine/video visits so practices can document and bill virtual encounters inside the same chart. Dedicated mobile apps let providers chart and billing staff track claims from iOS and Android devices.

On the analytics side, AdvancedMD includes built-in business intelligence dashboards for tracking collections, claim denials, and provider productivity, plus API and marketplace integrations for connecting labs, clearinghouses, and other health-IT systems to the core platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: The AdvancedMD Experience
paragraphs: Getting started with AdvancedMD typically involves a guided sales and implementation process rather than pure self-service signup, since pricing and configuration depend on which modules a practice licenses, how many providers are covered, and which specialty templates are needed. Prospective customers request a demo and custom quote through AdvancedMD's sales team.

Existing customers and independent reviewers generally rate AdvancedMD well for the depth of its billing and RCM tooling and for the responsiveness of phone-based support, while the most common complaints center on a steeper learning curve than lighter competitors and total costs that can climb as more modules or encounter-based billing services are added.

Because AdvancedMD has changed corporate ownership multiple times — most recently reverting to Francisco Partners in 2024 — the day-to-day product experience has stayed relatively stable, but buyers evaluating a multi-year contract may want to confirm current roadmap and support commitments directly with sales.

Modular pricing lets practices start with PM and billing, then add EHR later
Long operating history with established payer and clearinghouse connections

faqs.question: How much does AdvancedMD cost?
faqs.answer: AdvancedMD uses custom, quote-based pricing charged per provider per month, which varies depending on which modules (practice management, EHR, billing, telehealth) a practice selects. Publicly cited starting points run from roughly $429/provider/month for practice management plus billing up to around $729/provider/month for practice management plus EHR; some billing services are instead priced per claim or as a percentage of collections.

faqs.question: Is AdvancedMD an EHR or a practice management system?
faqs.answer: Both. AdvancedMD is a modular suite — practices can license practice management and medical billing on their own, or add the certified EHR and telehealth modules, all running on one shared patient and financial database.

faqs.question: Does AdvancedMD support telehealth?
faqs.answer: Yes, AdvancedMD includes native video visit functionality that lets providers document and bill telehealth encounters inside the same patient chart used for in-person visits.

faqs.question: Who owns AdvancedMD?
faqs.answer: AdvancedMD has changed hands several times: Francisco Partners acquired it in 2008, sold it to ADP in 2011, Global Payments acquired it in 2018, and Francisco Partners took ownership again in a 2024 deal.

faqs.question: Is AdvancedMD a good fit for medical billing companies?
faqs.answer: Yes — AdvancedMD is specifically built to let a single medical billing or RCM company manage claims, statements, and collections across many separate client practices from one account, which is one of its more distinctive strengths compared to single-practice-only EHR platforms.

enrichment.short_description: Adyen is a global payments platform giving enterprises a single integration for online, in-store, and platform payments worldwide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Adyen is a global payments technology company founded in 2006 and headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands. Publicly traded on Euronext Amsterdam since its 2018 IPO, Adyen has grown into one of the world's largest payment platforms, processing transactions for major international brands across e-commerce, retail, and platform businesses. Rather than stitching together a payment gateway, risk engine, and acquiring bank separately, Adyen built all three into one unified system.

That single-platform architecture is Adyen's core differentiator: merchants connect once and gain access to global acquiring, hundreds of local and international payment methods, fraud and risk tools, and unified reporting across every sales channel — online, in-app, and in-person point-of-sale. This end-to-end model appeals to businesses that operate across many countries and channels and want one data source, one integration, and one settlement process instead of managing multiple regional processors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Adyen Is For
paragraphs: Adyen is built for larger, growth-stage and enterprise businesses with meaningful transaction volume and international ambitions. It's a strong fit for global e-commerce retailers, omnichannel brands that need consistent payments across web, mobile, and physical stores, marketplaces and platforms that must split payouts to multiple sellers, and subscription or SaaS companies managing recurring billing at scale.

It also suits engineering-led organizations that want deep API access and control over the checkout and risk experience, rather than a purely off-the-shelf hosted page. Companies expanding into new markets benefit from Adyen's broad library of local payment methods and local acquiring licenses, which can improve authorization rates and reduce cross-border friction compared with a single-market processor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Adyen is generally not designed for solo entrepreneurs, hobby projects, or very small businesses processing a handful of transactions a month. Onboarding typically involves a sales conversation and an underwriting/KYC review rather than instant self-serve sign-up, which can feel heavy for a small shop that just wants to accept a few payments quickly.

Businesses that want fully transparent, flat, publicly listed pricing without any negotiation may also prefer a simpler processor, since Adyen's fees are usage-based and largely customized to volume and risk profile rather than published as a fixed rate card. Similarly, teams without engineering resources to integrate APIs or configure the platform may find lighter, more plug-and-play checkout providers a better match.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Adyen offers a unified commerce platform combining payment gateway, global acquiring, and risk management in one system, so merchants can accept cards, bank transfers, and dozens of local payment methods through a single integration. Adyen for Platforms extends this to marketplaces and software platforms that need to onboard sub-merchants and manage payouts and compliance on their behalf.

In-person payments are handled through Adyen's own point-of-sale terminals and hardware, letting retailers unify online and offline transaction data in one dashboard. Machine-learning-driven risk tools (marketed as RevenueProtect) aim to reduce fraud while minimizing false declines, and revenue optimization tools work to improve authorization rates through smart retry and routing logic.

Developers get REST APIs, SDKs for web and mobile, and drop-in UI components for building custom checkout flows, while finance and operations teams get consolidated reporting, reconciliation, and multi-currency settlement across all channels and markets.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Adyen Experience
paragraphs: Getting started with Adyen is a more guided, sales-assisted process than signing up for a self-serve payment button. Prospective merchants typically go through a discovery conversation, an underwriting/compliance review, and a technical integration phase supported by Adyen's implementation and developer-support teams, which suits enterprise procurement cycles but takes longer than an instant API key.

Once integrated, most users describe the developer experience as robust and well-documented, with a comprehensive dashboard (the Adyen Customer Area) that surfaces transaction-level detail, risk insights, and settlement reporting across all channels in one place. Ongoing support is typically delivered through a named account or technical support relationship rather than generic ticket queues, reflecting Adyen's focus on mid-market and enterprise clients over self-serve small businesses.

faqs.question: What is Adyen used for?
faqs.answer: Adyen is a payments platform that lets businesses accept online, in-app, and in-store payments through a single integration, covering global acquiring, local payment methods, and risk management.

faqs.question: How much does Adyen cost?
faqs.answer: Adyen uses usage-based pricing built around interchange-plus fees and a per-transaction processing fee, customized to a merchant's volume, markets, and risk profile rather than a published flat rate.

faqs.question: Does Adyen offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Adyen does not market a self-serve free trial in the way many smaller payment tools do; onboarding is typically handled through a sales and underwriting process tailored to the business.

faqs.question: Who typically uses Adyen?
faqs.answer: Adyen is commonly used by larger e-commerce retailers, omnichannel brands, marketplaces, platforms, and subscription businesses that operate across multiple countries and sales channels.

faqs.question: Is Adyen a bank or just a payment gateway?
faqs.answer: Adyen operates as its own acquirer in many regions in addition to providing gateway and risk-management services, which is part of what distinguishes its unified-platform approach from a pure gateway provider.
